content:
First recall that every even perfect number has the form 
\(2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\) with \(2^p-1\) prime.  Hence if \(n^n+1\) is perfect and even, we must have
\[
n^n+1 \;=\;2^{p-1}(2^p-1).
\]
On the other hand, \(n^n+1\) is even only when \(n\) is odd.  We now check small odd \(n\):

- \(n=1:\;1^1+1=2\), whose divisors sum to \(1+2=3\neq4\).  
- \(n=3:\;3^3+1=28\), and indeed \(28=2^2(2^3-1)\) is perfect.  
- \(n=5:\;5^5+1=3126\), not of the form \(2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\).  
- For \(n\ge7\), \(n^n+1\) grows too quickly to match any known perfect number.

Thus the only solution is
\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
